question: What are Streams in Dart? Explain single-subscription vs broadcast streams.
answer:
A Stream<T> delivers a sequence of async events over time. Like an async iterator.

Single-subscription (default):
• Only ONE listener at a time
• Can pause/resume the producer
• Use for: file reading, HTTP body, socket

Broadcast:
• MULTIPLE simultaneous listeners
• Cannot pause producer
• Use for: user input events, pub/sub, ChangeNotifier-style

StreamController — creates and exposes a stream you can push events into.
async* generator — simplest way to create streams with yield.

question: How do you use StreamController and explain stream transformations?
answer:
StreamController gives you a stream + a sink to push events into.

Transformations (all return a new Stream):
• map() — transform each event
• where() — filter events
• take(n) / skip(n) — limit or skip events
• distinct() — skip duplicate consecutive events
• debounceTime() — wait for silence (rxdart)
• throttle() — rate limit
• switchMap() — cancel previous inner stream when new event arrives
• asyncMap() — map with async operations
• expand() — one-to-many transform
• scan() — accumulate (like fold but streaming)

question: What is StreamSubscription and how do you manage its lifecycle correctly?
answer:
Calling `stream.listen(...)` returns a StreamSubscription. It is the handle to the active subscription — keep it, or you can't stop, pause, or cancel.

Methods:
• `cancel()` — stop receiving events; for single-subscription streams, frees the producer too
• `pause([Future resumeSignal])` — pause delivery; events buffer or backpressure depending on the source
• `resume()` — resume delivery
• `onData / onError / onDone` — replace the handlers post-construction

Lifecycle rules:
• If the State that owns the subscription is unmounted, the subscription must be cancelled in dispose() — otherwise the callback fires on a dead state and crashes (or, worse, leaks objects via a closure over `this`)
• `cancel()` is idempotent and returns Future<void> — wait for it if you need to be sure cleanup ran
• On a single-subscription stream, cancel propagates to the producer (e.g. async* generator stops at its current yield, file handle closes)
• On a broadcast stream, your cancel only removes you — other subscribers and the producer continue

A common bug: using `stream.listen(onData)` and forgetting the return value — you can't cancel later. Always assign to a field if the subscription needs to outlive the listen call.

question: When do you use `await for` vs `listen` on a stream?
answer:
Two ways to consume a stream — both delegate to the same subscription mechanism, but they shape your code very differently.

`await for (final x in stream) { ... }`:
• Sequential — the loop body MUST complete before the next event is delivered
• Backpressure — if your loop body is slow, the producer is paused (single-subscription) or events buffer (broadcast)
• Easy to read; reads like a for-loop
• Cannot easily attach error handlers other than try/catch around the loop
• Use when the consumer needs to do bounded work per event in order

`stream.listen(onData)`:
• Reactive — onData fires per event; your `listen` returns immediately
• Events arrive even if the previous handler hasn't finished — you handle concurrency yourself
• Lets you pass `onError`, `onDone`, `cancelOnError`
• Must keep the subscription handle for cancel/pause
• Use when you want fine-grained control or async handlers that should run concurrently

Gotchas:
• `await for` on a broadcast stream is fine but you can't easily share that loop with another consumer — listen is more flexible for fan-out
• `await for` cannot be cancelled from outside — break out of the loop on a sentinel event
• Inside an async* function, `await for` paired with `yield` lets you transform a stream cleanly

question: How do you handle errors in a Stream pipeline?
answer:
Errors on a stream are first-class events delivered through the error channel — they don't throw at the listen call site. The handler chain offers several places to react.

Where errors land:
• `stream.listen(onData, onError: ...)` — handler receives (Object error, StackTrace stack)
• `stream.handleError((e, s) {...})` — pipeline operator that catches and either swallows or rethrows
• `await for` — wrap in try/catch; if cancelOnError is true (default), the iteration stops on first error
• `transformer` — convert errors into typed events (e.g. wrap into `Result.err`)

Cancel-on-error:
• Default for `await for` and `Stream.first`
• Default for `stream.listen` is `cancelOnError: false` — loop continues; pass true if you want it to stop on first error

Design tips:
• For a Bloc/Riverpod pipeline, transform errors INTO state. Don't let the stream simply close on a transient network blip — emit `Failed(e)` and let the next user action retry.
• Use `handleError` to scope which errors the rest of the pipeline sees. Common pattern: `handleError((e) { if (e is FormatException) ...; else throw e; })`
• Don't `print` — emit to your logger and decide whether to rethrow

Gotcha: a thrown synchronous error inside an async* function becomes an error event on the resulting stream. If your generator throws inside a loop, surrounding code that does `listen(onError: ...)` catches it; surrounding code that does `await for` catches it via try/catch.

question: What does rxdart bring on top of dart:async — BehaviorSubject, switchMap, debounce?
answer:
rxdart wraps dart:async with the Rx-style operators that are standard in other reactive libraries (RxJava, RxJS). The core stream model is the same — rxdart's classes are still Streams — but it gives you operators that are tedious to hand-roll.

BehaviorSubject<T>:
• Like a broadcast StreamController that always replays the LAST value to a new listener
• Useful for state-like streams: a UI that subscribes mid-flow still sees the current value
• Combined with `.value` getter for synchronous reads

ReplaySubject<T>:
• Replays the last N (or all) values to new listeners
• Useful for event logs and stateful caches

Key operators not in dart:async:
• `debounceTime` — emit only after a period of silence (great for search input)
• `throttleTime` — emit at most once per period
• `switchMap` — when a new event arrives, cancel the in-flight inner stream from the previous event (search-as-you-type cancellation pattern)
• `combineLatest` / `combineLatest2`...— combine the latest values from multiple streams into one
• `startWith(value)` — prepend an initial value
• `withLatestFrom` — pair each event with the latest from another stream
• `bufferTime` / `windowTime` — group events by time window

When NOT to add rxdart:
• You're using Riverpod and most operators have equivalents in providers + Notifiers — extra dependency may not be worth it
• Single-screen, tiny pipeline — handcraft the missing operator with async*

Gotcha: BehaviorSubject is broadcast — multiple listeners share the same subscription. Closing the subject is on you.

question: How do you combine multiple streams — merge, zip, combineLatest?
answer:
Three combinators cover most multi-stream patterns. Same names show up in RxDart, RxJS, and dart:async helpers.

merge:
• Forward EVERY event from EVERY input stream, in arrival order
• Use when: "show any error from any source" / "collect events from multiple chats"
• `Rx.merge([a, b, c])` (rxdart), or `StreamGroup.merge` (dart:async)

zip:
• Pair the i-th event of each input. Output is `[a₀, b₀, c₀]`, then `[a₁, b₁, c₁]`, ...
• If one input runs out, output stops — slowest input is the bottleneck
• Use when: "render output i = f(streamA[i], streamB[i])"
• `Rx.zip2(a, b, (x, y) => ...)` etc.

combineLatest:
• Whenever ANY input emits, emit a tuple of the LATEST value from each input
• Output starts only when EVERY input has emitted at least once
• Use when: "derived state — recompute when any input changes"
• `Rx.combineLatest2/3/...`

Other useful combinators (rxdart):
• `concat([a, b, c])` — drain a, then b, then c (sequential)
• `withLatestFrom(other)` — emit when source emits, paired with other's latest
• `startWith(value)` — prepend a seed value

For Riverpod users: most of this can be expressed with derived providers (each `ref.watch` is a combineLatest under the hood). For BLoC, rxdart's combinators are the standard tool.

question: What is backpressure and how do single-subscription vs broadcast streams handle it?
answer:
Backpressure is the question "what happens when the producer outpaces the consumer?". Streams behave very differently depending on type and consumption mode.

Single-subscription stream:
• Producer is paused when the consumer pauses or is slow inside `await for`
• `pause(Future)` on the subscription tells the producer to stop until the future completes
• Built-in backpressure — the producer's `controller.add(...)` blocks (or buffers, depending on controller config) when paused
• Examples that respect this naturally: HTTP body streams, file IO, async generators that `await` between yields

Broadcast stream:
• NO backpressure — the producer doesn't know about specific listeners and can't pause itself
• Each listener sees every event when it's emitted; if the listener can't keep up, events queue inside the engine and memory grows
• Use this for fast, small events (UI events, pubsub messages) — never for high-volume data streams

When you have a fast producer and slow consumer:
• Convert the source to a single-subscription stream (don't use broadcast just for convenience)
• Buffer or window — `bufferTime`, `bufferCount` — collapse N events into one batch your consumer can process
• Sample / throttle — drop intermediate events you can ignore (cursor positions, sensor data)
• Sink-based push with a bounded queue — apply your own quota and drop or coalesce when full

Common mistake: using a `StreamController.broadcast()` for an event log that fans out to dozens of subscribers, while one subscriber writes to disk synchronously. Memory grows during disk slow-downs because broadcast has no backpressure. The fix is either (a) a faster consumer, (b) a per-listener bounded queue, or (c) a different fan-out mechanism (a database with notifications).

question: How do you decide between Stream and Future for an API?
answer:
Future = ONE event at SOME point. Stream = ZERO-or-more events over time. The choice shapes everything from your error handling to your testing.

Return a Future when:
• The operation has a single result — fetch a user, post a form, read a file
• The caller wants to compose with `await`
• Errors are exceptional (a thrown error stops the awaited expression)

Return a Stream when:
• The operation produces multiple values over time — websocket messages, sensor readings, paginated results yielded as you fetch them
• The caller wants to react incrementally — partial UI rendering, progress indicators
• Backpressure or pause/resume matters — file streaming, video frames

Paginated APIs are a great test:
• Fetch one page → return Future<Page<T>> (caller calls again for the next page; explicit)
• Iterate ALL items → return Stream<T> (consumer can `await for` and stop when satisfied; built-in backpressure)
• Both can be useful in the same codebase; choose by the consumer's needs

When in doubt, prefer Future. Streams are more powerful but more complex — you commit your callers to subscription management. Streams that emit exactly one event are usually a sign you should have returned a Future.

Mixing the two:
• Future.asStream() — promote a Future into a single-event stream (rarely needed)
• Stream.first / Stream.last — convert a stream's first/last event into a Future
• Stream.toList() — drain the whole stream into a Future<List<T>> (only when you know it's bounded)
